title: Japanese GP 2026: Practice Sessions Analysis & Race Predictions
summary: Mercedes dominated the Suzuka practice weekend with Kimi Antonelli topping aggregated times in two of three sessions, while our exclusive data reveals major untapped potential in Gabriel Bortoleto and Oliver Bearman. With aggregated lap times predicting a 1:29.3 pole battle, the Silver Arrows look formidable heading into qualifying.

sections.heading: Mercedes Set the Pace at Suzuka
sections.content:
Suzuka delivered a gripping practice weekend with Mercedes stamping their authority across all three sessions. Kimi Antonelli emerged as the man to beat, topping the aggregated standings in both FP1 and FP3 with a blistering 1:29.325 ultimate pace in the final session — and crucially, his actual best lap of 1:29.362 was only 0.037s slower, signalling exceptional consistency from the young Italian. George Russell was never far behind, clocking a 1:29.346 aggregated time in FP3 to keep the Mercedes 1-2 locked in.

Oscar Piastri threw a curveball in FP2, leading both aggregated and actual standings with a 1:30.133 that perfectly matched his single-lap best — a rare case of a driver nailing every sector on the same lap. McLaren's pace looked genuine in the middle session, though they slipped behind Ferrari in FP3 as Leclerc and Hamilton found their rhythm on the low-fuel runs.

The competitive order became progressively clearer through the weekend. Mercedes held a comfortable advantage of nearly seven-tenths over Ferrari in FP3 aggregated pace, with McLaren a further tenth behind. Red Bull's struggles continued with Verstappen managing only 9th in FP3, over 1.5 seconds adrift — a deficit that will alarm the Milton Keynes squad. The midfield battle, however, produced genuine intrigue, with Audi's Bortoleto and Hulkenberg showing flashes of top-six potential and Racing Bulls' Liam Lawson putting together consistent laps through the technical Suzuka layout.

Our aggregated vs actual analysis reveals fascinating hidden stories that traditional timing screens miss entirely. Several drivers left significant time on the table across their best sectors, suggesting the qualifying order could look quite different once everyone strings together a clean lap under pressure.

sections.heading: FP3 Ultimate Pace — Aggregated Best Sectors
sections.description: Exclusive data combining each driver's best individual sectors across all FP3 laps, revealing true potential beyond the official timesheets

sections.heading: The Aggregated vs Actual Story: Where the Real Insights Hide
sections.content:
Standard F1 coverage reports the fastest single lap from each session — what we call the "actual" time. But our aggregated analysis takes each driver's personal best Sector 1, best Sector 2, and best Sector 3 from all their laps in a session and combines them. These sectors often come from completely different laps: a driver might set their best S1 on lap 7, best S2 on lap 14, and best S3 on lap 19. The resulting aggregated time represents the theoretical perfect lap — what they could achieve if they nailed every sector on the same tour.

Why does this matter? In qualifying, drivers push to the absolute limit and often do combine their best sector performance into a single lap. That makes aggregated practice times a significantly better predictor of qualifying pace than actual practice bests. The delta between aggregated and actual also tells a story about consistency: a small delta means the driver is already putting it together cleanly, while a large delta suggests untapped speed that could surface under qualifying pressure.

In FP3 at Suzuka, Antonelli's delta was a minuscule 0.037 seconds — he is already operating at near-perfect consistency. Contrast that with Bortoleto's +0.383s gap or Bearman's massive +0.482s delta, and you see drivers with genuine pace trapped inside inconsistent laps. Hulkenberg, interestingly, posted an identical aggregated and actual time — a perfect lap, but his 1:30.658 suggests the Audi is simply lacking the raw pace of the frontrunners. This kind of analysis is exclusive to our platform and unavailable on F1.com or any official timing source.

sections.heading: Hidden Potential: FP3 Aggregated vs Actual Comparison
sections.description: The gap between ultimate potential and single-lap delivery — larger deltas mean more time left on the table

sections.heading: Sector-by-Sector: Where the Lap Time Is Won and Lost
sections.content:
Suzuka's three sectors each demand something different, and the FP3 sector data paints a vivid picture of where each team's strengths lie. Sector 1 — the high-speed esses and Dunlop curve — was Russell's domain with a 32.029, nearly a tenth faster than Antonelli's 32.159. Russell's superior high-speed confidence through those direction changes was evident, yet he couldn't carry that advantage through the rest of the lap.

Sector 2, which runs from Degner through the hairpin and up through Spoon, belonged to Antonelli with a 39.646 — almost three-hundredths quicker than Russell's 39.675. Ferrari's Leclerc was notably competitive here with a 40.078, nearly two-tenths adrift but closer than the overall gap suggested. McLaren's Piastri also impressed in S2 with 39.869, hinting at strong mechanical grip through the medium-speed corners.

The final sector — the fast 130R through the chicane to the line — saw Antonelli again lead with a 17.520, though Leclerc's 17.616 and Lawson's 17.624 kept the gap tight. Lindblad's 17.622 in S3 deserves special mention: the Racing Bulls rookie matched his more experienced teammate and outpaced several top-team drivers through the trickiest braking zone on the circuit. Verstappen's sector times tell a story of a car that's simply lacking downforce everywhere: 9th in S1 (32.659), 11th in S2 (40.457), and 7th in S3 (17.712). There's no single area of weakness to address — it's a fundamental deficit.

sections.caption: Suzuka International Racing Course — Sector 1 covers the iconic esses and Dunlop curve, Sector 2 runs through Degner, the hairpin, and Spoon, while Sector 3 encompasses 130R and the chicane

sections.heading: Tyre Strategy: Soft Compound in Favour, McLaren Skip the Hards
sections.content:
The tyre data reveals a clear lean toward the soft compound across the grid, but the detail lies in how each team approached their allocation. Mercedes ran the most aggressive programme: Russell churned through 10 sets in total (6 soft, 1 medium, 3 hard), while Antonelli used 9 sets (5 soft, 2 medium, 2 hard). The heavy hard-tyre running from Mercedes suggests they are seriously evaluating a strategy involving the hardest compound for race day — potentially a medium-hard or even a soft-hard two-stop approach.

McLaren stand out as the only top team to completely skip the hard compound. Both Norris (4 soft, 2 medium) and Piastri (4 soft, 3 medium) focused exclusively on the two softer compounds, implying they have either written off the hard for race day or gathered enough data from long runs on the medium to feel confident. Ferrari also kept things economical with 6 sets per driver (4 soft, 1 medium, 1 hard), a focused allocation that suggests they came to Suzuka with a clear programme and didn't deviate.

Audi's tyre usage was notably aggressive. Hulkenberg led the midfield with 9 total sets (6 soft), while Bortoleto used 8 sets (5 soft). This heavy soft-compound usage points to the team chasing single-lap performance to understand their car's limits rather than conserving tyres for race strategy simulations. Red Bull's conservative approach — just 6 sets for Verstappen and 5 for Hadjar — might explain their lack of representative pace; fewer runs mean fewer opportunities to find the setup window.

sections.heading: Tyre Compound Usage — All Sessions Combined
sections.description: Total sets used across FP1, FP2, FP3 and qualifying for all drivers

sections.heading: Session Evolution: The Weekend Build-Up
sections.content:
The progression from FP1 through FP3 told a clear story of Mercedes tightening their grip. In FP1, Antonelli's aggregated best of 1:31.328 led Russell by just 0.061 seconds, with Ferrari's Leclerc 0.337s behind in third. The gaps were relatively compressed, with the top six covered by 0.644 seconds. Russell actually topped the actual standings in FP1 (1:31.666), suggesting Antonelli was still calibrating his approach.

FP2 saw Piastri seize the initiative with a 1:30.133 that was identical to his actual best — a perfect lap in the middle of the afternoon. Antonelli was just 0.011s behind in aggregated pace, and the McLaren-Mercedes battle appeared finely balanced. Lap times dropped by over a second from FP1 as track evolution kicked in and the teams switched to softer compounds for qualifying simulations.

By FP3, Mercedes had found another gear entirely. Antonelli's 1:29.325 aggregated time represented a further 0.8s improvement, and the gap back to third-placed Leclerc ballooned to 0.687 seconds. The interesting subplot was Red Bull's relative improvement: Verstappen moved from 1:32.639 in FP1 (1.311s off) to 1:30.828 in FP3 (1.503s off in aggregated terms) — they found raw speed but the frontrunners pulled away even faster. Audi's trajectory was quietly impressive, with both drivers cracking the top eight in FP3 after languishing in 10th-12th territory during FP1.

sections.heading: Key Findings and Surprises
sections.content:
1. Mercedes dominance is multifaceted. It's not just raw speed — Antonelli's 0.037s aggregated-to-actual delta in FP3 shows a driver and car operating in remarkable harmony. Russell has more to find (0.270s delta), which makes the qualifying battle between them fascinating: if Russell strings it together, a sub-1:29.1 pole time is within reach.

2. Audi's quiet emergence is the story of the midfield. Bortoleto's aggregated 1:30.617 in FP3 puts him within range of Hamilton's Ferrari, and his 0.383s delta means there's real speed trapped in that car. Hulkenberg's perfectly executed lap (zero delta) confirms the C in their car is approaching its limit — but that limit is getting closer to the frontrunners each weekend.

3. Red Bull are in genuine trouble. Verstappen's 1.5-second deficit to pole pace at a circuit where aerodynamic efficiency is critical exposes fundamental car weaknesses. The conservative tyre programme (only 6 sets) suggests they may also be managing budget or parts concerns rather than pushing for performance answers.

4. Oliver Bearman's 0.482s delta is the largest in the top 15. The young Haas driver set individual sector times competitive with the lower midfield, but couldn't string them together. This is classic rookie inconsistency at a challenging circuit, and qualifying could see a significant jump if he delivers a clean lap.

5. The Racing Bulls pair showed genuine pace in S3, with Lindblad's 17.622 rivalling established names. The team's strong braking performance into the chicane could make them tricky customers in qualifying and race situations alike.

sections.heading: Race Day Predictions
sections.content:
Qualifying Prediction: Based on aggregated data, Antonelli is the favourite for pole with a predicted lap in the 1:29.0-1:29.2 range. Russell should be right alongside — his 0.270s delta means he has significant time to unlock, and qualifying conditions tend to bring out the best from the Mercedes driver. Leclerc is the most likely challenger for the front row if either Silver Arrow falters, while Piastri's proven ability to nail a perfect lap (as seen in FP2) makes him a contender for P3 or P4.

Race Prediction: Mercedes' heavy hard-tyre running hints at a strategic advantage on Sunday. While McLaren have looked potent in single-lap trim, their decision to skip the hard compound entirely could limit their strategic flexibility. A two-stop soft-medium-hard strategy could play into Mercedes' hands, especially if tyre degradation on the medium is higher than expected. Ferrari's conservative tyre programme suggests confidence in a straightforward strategy, but their pace deficit of nearly seven-tenths in FP3 may be too much to overcome on pure speed.

Podium Prediction:
1. Kimi Antonelli (Mercedes) — pace, consistency, and strategic preparation all point his way
2. George Russell (Mercedes) — will push his teammate hard but may come up just short
3. Oscar Piastri (McLaren) — strong race craft and the fastest actual lap in FP2 suggest he'll make positions on Sunday

Wildcard Factors: Suzuka's mix of high-speed corners and heavy braking zones means safety cars triggered by midfield incidents are common. If a late safety car bunches the field, McLaren's medium-tyre expertise could prove decisive in a sprint to the flag. Weather could also play a role — any rain would completely reset the order.

Dark Horse: Gabriel Bortoleto. With a 0.383s delta in FP3, the Audi rookie has genuine pace to unlock. If he delivers a clean qualifying lap, a top-eight start is realistic, and his soft-heavy strategy could translate into an aggressive opening stint on race day.

Key Battle to Watch: Antonelli vs Russell for the win. The aggregated data says just 0.021 seconds separate them — the tightest intra-team gap on the grid. If Russell closes his delta, this could be one of the great teammate battles at Suzuka.
